comments.comment_body: How do we get this in a windows system?
comments.post_body:
I built a solution to reduce cognitive load when working with multiple LLMs

The problem: You ask ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini the same question, then mentally synthesize three different answers.

The solution: "Merge mode" for PolyGPT

How it works:
 - Log into AI providers in split-screen (no API keys needed)
 - Ask your question once
 - All LLMs respond simultaneously
 - 4th window shows an AI-synthesized answer with citations [1] [2] [3]
 - Follow-up questions continue the merged conversation

Instead of reading three responses, you get one coherent answer combining the best of all models.

Inspired by Andrej Karpathy's "LLM council" concept, adapted for web-based LLMs.

Built on open source PolyGPT. Mac executable + code coming soon.

How do you handle comparing LLM responses?

#AI #LLM #ProductivityTools #OpenSource
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about a solution to reduce cognitive load when working with multiple Large Language Models (LLMs), which allows users to merge responses from different LLMs into a single, coherent answer. The solution, called 'Merge mode' for PolyGPT, enables users to ask a question once and receive a synthesized answer from multiple LLMs.

comments.comment_body:
Aketh T.M No pre-built Windows binaries yet. You can install Node.js, then: git clone https://github.com/sabyasachis/merged-polygpt.git && cd merged-polygpt && npm install && npm start

 Or run npm run build to create a Windows .exe.

comments.comment_body:
In five years, we will know.

Many here point that Java didn’t kill anything, but Rust unlike Java has no GC.
comments.post_body:
Comparing programming in C/C++ and Rust is like comparing driving on the roads of the 70s/80s to driving on today’s highways. Nowadays, we have better roads, vehicles designed to absorb impact energy, ABS brakes, airbags, mandatory seatbelts. All of this has reduced road accidents by 70–80%. Starting a new project in C/C++ is like expecting an old Dyane 6 to get you to your destination the same way a Tesla would. Drive safely and code in Rust.

Or perhaps you'd rather read it in the words of Mark Russinovich:
comments.context_summary: The commenter is comparing programming in C/C++ to Rust, arguing that Rust is a safer and more modern choice, similar to how modern cars are safer than older models. They quote Mark Russinovich in support of their argument, emphasizing the importance of prioritizing safety in coding.

comments.comment_body: It may take more than 5 years, Aketh. But Rust introduces a significant paradigm shift with its ownership and borrow checker system, which may potentially redefine how memory is managed and safety is ensured in software development.

comments.comment_body: If you do know max amount of data you are likely to push into the array, you could hardcode it as well
comments.post_body:
While going through the DSA fundamentals on NeetCode, I stumbled upon something really cool: when a dynamic array runs out of space, it doesn’t just add one more slot - it doubles its entire capacity!

Imagine this: instead of constantly resizing every time you add an element (which would be super slow), the array says, “Hey, I need more space!” and allocates twice the amount it currently has.

This seemingly simple idea is genius:

- Amortized O(1) insertion - most additions are super fast

- Reduced memory allocations - fewer expensive resize operations

- Predictable growth pattern - capacity grows as 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32…


It's one of those "obvious in hindsight" optimizations that make you appreciate the thought that goes into fundamental data structures. Sometimes the most elegant solutions hide in plain sight!


#DataStructures #Algorithms #SoftwareEngineering #Programming
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the efficiency of dynamic arrays doubling their capacity when running out of space, allowing for amortized O(1) insertion and reduced memory allocations. The commenter is highlighting this optimization as a fundamental yet elegant solution in data structures.

comments.comment_body: Portland rain isn't very different from Seattle rain. Just a bit warmer in Portland.
comments.post_body:
Candidates are always shocked when I’m like “you SURE you want to move to Redmond, WA!?” It rains. It rains a lot. I lived in Seattle and loved it, but it’s def a shock for people. Some people love it. Others hate it. The two places I don’t see people move from as often are CA and NY. 

East coast / west coast culture shock 

Mixed with why would you leave sunny San Diego?!

I’m not in the business of selling people on the idea or pushing people to relocate. It’s my least favorite thing to do. If you want to relocate, GREAT. Otherwise idc and I completely understand not wanting to uproot your life.

I think relocating people is also damaging in some ways. Drives housing costs, people don’t stay long, and there’s a longer time line to make that transition. Easier to recruit in the market you’re in than relocate people!
comments.context_summary: The commenter is discussing the challenges of relocating to Redmond, WA, particularly for those from sunny areas like California, and expressing their reluctance to recruit or convince people to move. They believe recruiting locally is often easier and less damaging than relocating people, which can drive up housing costs and lead to other negative consequences.

comments.comment_body: The eco-friendly design has put the garden back into the garden city for sure.
comments.post_body:
As someone who's now working from Google's Ananta campus in Bengaluru, I can honestly say it's a game-changer. The thoughtful design and focus on sustainability create a truly unique work environment.

𝐈𝐧𝐬𝐩𝐢𝐫𝐞𝐝 𝐛𝐲 𝐍𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞: The integration of green spaces and walking paths throughout the campus is a constant source of inspiration and a welcome break from the screen.

𝐄𝐜𝐨-𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐜𝐢𝐨𝐮𝐬 𝐃𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧: The 100% wastewater recycling is a testament to Google's commitment to a sustainable future, and it's something I value deeply.
A Boost for India: Knowing that the building materials were primarily sourced locally makes me feel even more connected to the positive impact of this project.

𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐏𝐨𝐰𝐞𝐫 𝐨𝐟 "𝐀𝐧𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐚": The name, meaning "infinite," perfectly reflects the feeling of boundless opportunities here.

𝐀 𝐕𝐢𝐛𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐭 𝐄𝐜𝐨𝐬𝐲𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐦: The sheer scale of the campus (1.6 million sq ft!) and the presence of thousands of talented engineers create a dynamic and energizing atmosphere.

Ananta is more than just a workplace; it's a place that fosters creativity, collaboration, and a sense of purpose. I encourage everyone to seek out work environments that prioritize both productivity and well-being – it makes all the difference! 

#GoogleAnanta #WorkplaceWellbeing #Productivity #Innovation #Bengaluru #Tech #Sustainability #Google #India #Inspired
comments.context_summary: The commenter is sharing their positive experience working at Google's Ananta campus in Bengaluru, highlighting its sustainable design, unique work environment, and positive impact on productivity and well-being. They encourage others to seek out similar work environments that prioritize both productivity and well-being.

comments.comment_body: Intel and TSMC would be the real shovel sellers.
comments.post_body:
90% of software engineers don't realize that they should be applying the Nvidia model to their own work.

Nvidia is a brilliant business as it doesn't care if individual AI plays succeed or fail. As long as there's some hitters, the space grows and they make money.

The beautiful thing is that you can integrate this exact same concept into the code you write.

Instead of writing code to push a solitary feature that you own, write code that empowers other engineers to write code to push their awesome features.

This is a huge reason why so many engineers flee product teams to switch into infra teams, especially when pushing for Staff+ levels at Big Tech.

Rockstar engineers land multiplicative impact instead of individual impact. To learn how to do that and understand the complexities of product vs. infra, check out our in-depth guide here: https://lnkd.in/dgGW_44i

#techcareergrowth #nvidia #promotion #staffengineer #growthtips
comments.context_summary: The post discusses how software engineers can apply the Nvidia model to their work, focusing on writing code that empowers others to create features rather than individual ownership, and how this approach can lead to multiplicative impact and career growth. The commenter is responding to the idea that this mindset shift can help engineers achieve Staff+ levels at Big Tech companies.

comments.comment_body: Yup. Especially in a globalized world where it’s easy for people to misinterpret ours and everyone else”s behaviors and signals.
comments.post_body:
IIT made me feel I wasn’t that “intelligent”. With an All India Rank of 1720, I took Metallurgy and anyone who’s gone to IIT knows that Metallurgy students are seen “not as smart” as Computer Science or Electrical, or even Mechanical ones. 

To add perspective, every year 1,500,000 students appear for engineering exams in India. So at 1720, I was in top 0.001 and yet I felt stupid because I wasn’t the best in Maths and Science. 

I was a writer, an impeccable orator and an artist. I had self awareness, I could empathise with people and communicate my thoughts effectively. But who cares? Those are “soft skills” right? None of that counted as “intelligence”. 

I find it unsettling how our schooling system completely ignores core competencies like emotional intelligence and communication skills. And we grow up with this skewed definition of intelligence. It’s almost a scam. The moment you leave college and enter a profession, you realise that work is all about people (At least for the vast majority) 
You are building for people, working with people. 

Your soft skills are not nice-to-haves. They are key to professional growth. I wish at 18, someone told this to me and I paid more attention to hone my soft skills. 

What do you think? Do share your thoughts. 

#education #india #iit
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about how the Indian education system, particularly IIT, prioritizes technical skills over soft skills like emotional intelligence and communication, and how this narrow definition of intelligence can be limiting. The original post shares a personal experience of feeling inadequate despite being a high achiever, and highlights the importance of soft skills in professional growth.

comments.comment_body: Where r the pictures/Ai for us to try?
comments.post_body:
The colors of the family tree

Have you looked through old B&W pictures and wondered what the world looked like back then?

There's a certain resonance to shades of gray but once in a while it evokes curiosity.

The Singapore government worked on a project to convert old B&W images into color

They wanted to keep the texture and feel of the old pictures.

However, they're not using artists to do the job. This would not have been a must-do project

Apart from the cost and time this would entail, it was not practical to do manually.

So they used a bank of images to train the AI to work its magic on the palette

You can see the results, both the successful and the failed ones in the links below.

It worked best on high resolution pictures featuring humans and natural scenery.

Not the saturated, glossy and detailed millions of colors you see in images today.

May be that's for the best.

A colorful but slightly faded version of the world then

Not just in sepia

The project is now open for anyone to upload and experiment with their old B&W images

Are there family pictures you'd like to see in color?

#brands #behavioraleconomics #startups
comments.context_summary: The post discusses a Singapore government project that uses AI to convert old black and white images into color, and the commenter is responding to the project's results and the opportunity for others to upload and experiment with their own old photos. The project aims to preserve the texture and feel of old pictures while adding color to them.

comments.comment_body: Moral of the story - do what works for you.
comments.post_body:
As someone whose job at LinkedIn is to help seekers get hired, I feel compelled to write this post. Do *not* do this — do not put all your eggs in one basket (or 5).

I do not mean this as a criticism of Taylor's post: I think it's well intentioned & you're absolutely likely to increase your odds of landing a job if you spend time researching the company, making connections, etc (and that's what LinkedIn is great for!) However, job seeking is hard and there's a lot of variables that you wouldn't have accounted for.

Personal story: prior to LinkedIn, I was pretty close to joining Trello. The interviews had gone really well & I'd pretty much paused the rest of my job search. Then they first went cold before turning me down. No "we went with someone else". It's only later did I come to know that since Atlassian decided to shutter Hipchat, they had paused external hiring for a bit (to ensure they could move folks around internally).

It hurt to not get the role, but worse, I didn't have a pipeline. Please keep your options open at all times, no matter how well you think you're prepared & how well you've done in your interviews. Worst case scenario you'll have some leverage in negotiations. But keep searching until you've signed your offer & started your new role.

And all the best!
comments.context_summary: The commenter, a LinkedIn employee, is advising job seekers not to put all their hopes into one job opportunity, sharing a personal story of a failed job application and emphasizing the importance of keeping options open until a new role is secured. They are responding to a post that suggested spending time researching a company and making connections can increase one's chances of landing a job.

comments.post_body: We're hiring SDEs in Bellevue/Seattle, Chicago, Geneva and Shenzhen.  Let me know if you or anyone you know wants to  build the next generation travel platform. Help us connect travelers to the places they want to go!
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a job posting for software development engineers at various locations to work on a next-generation travel platform. The post invites interested individuals to reach out and be a part of building a platform that connects travelers to their desired destinations.

comments.comment_body: Base condition until one of them goes out of business.
comments.post_body:
Want to understand RECURSION ?

2018 - Walmart buys Flipkart

2020 - Flipkart buys Walmart India

Full circle ;)

And don't ask me the BASE condition, as if 2020 has been any less !

#vish_speak #flipkart #walmartlabs  #walmart #IT
comments.context_summary: The post discusses the concept of recursion using the example of Walmart and Flipkart's business transactions, where Walmart bought Flipkart in 2018 and Flipkart later bought Walmart India in 2020, creating a full circle. The commenter is highlighting the recursive nature of this business deal.
